trialDesign.fullName: SCALE Obesity and Prediabetes — Liraglutide 3.0 mg for Weight Management
trialDesign.population: 3,731 adults without diabetes; BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with dyslipidemia or hypertension
trialDesign.duration: 56 weeks main trial; 12-week switch-to-placebo substudy in liraglutide subgroup
trialDesign.intervention: Liraglutide 3.0 mg SC once daily (titrated from 0.6 mg) plus lifestyle counseling
trialDesign.primaryEndpoint: Change in body weight; proportion achieving ≥5% and >10% weight loss at 56 weeks
trialDesign.overview: Liraglutide 3.0 mg produced -8.4 kg mean weight loss vs -2.8 kg placebo, with superior cardiometabolic improvements and delayed diabetes onset.

headings.background: Obesity is chronic and difficult to treat with lifestyle alone; liraglutide 3.0 mg targets weight via appetite reduction.
headings.design: 3731 patients, 2:1 randomization, 191 sites, 27 countries; double-blind; lifestyle counseling in both arms.
headings.population: 78.5% women; 61.2% prediabetes at baseline; no type 1 or 2 diabetes.
headings.intervention: Liraglutide 3.0 mg with weekly dose escalation from 0.6 mg.
headings.followUp: 56 weeks; LOCF for missing data; 9.9% liraglutide vs 3.8% placebo withdrew for AEs.

safetyHighlights: Nausea most common; peaks weeks 4–8 during titration
Gallbladder events 2.5% vs 1.0%; resting pulse +2.4 beats/min; no medullary thyroid carcinoma
trialSummary.clinicalBottomLine: Liraglutide 3.0 mg as adjunct to diet and exercise produces clinically meaningful weight loss and cardiometabolic benefit in obese adults without diabetes.

inclusionCriteria: Age ≥18 years; BMI ≥30, or BMI 27–29.9 with treated/untreated dyslipidemia or hypertension
exclusionCriteria: History of major depressive or other severe psychiatric disorders
Previous obesity pharmacotherapy within 3 months (protocol-specific exclusions)
keyTakeaways: Weight: -8.4 kg (8.0%) liraglutide vs -2.8 kg (2.6%) placebo at 56 weeks.
63.2% lost ≥5% weight; improved BP, lipids, glycemia; delayed T2DM onset.
Safe overall; nausea, gallbladder events, mild pulse increase; dose titration 0.6→3.0 mg.

items.title: SCALE Obesity and Prediabetes — Main Points (High-Yield Overview)
items.content:
Background and Rationale: Obesity drives type 2 diabetes prevalence, but sustained weight loss through lifestyle alone is difficult. Liraglutide 3.0 mg once daily reduces appetite and energy intake for weight management.

Study Design: 56-week, double-blind RCT in 3,731 patients without diabetes (BMI ≥30, or ≥27 with dyslipidemia/hypertension). Randomized 2:1 to liraglutide 3.0 mg vs placebo plus lifestyle counseling.

Weight Loss Efficacy: Liraglutide -8.4 kg (8.0%) vs placebo -2.8 kg (2.6%). ≥5% weight loss: 63.2% vs 27.1%; >10%: 33.1% vs 10.6%.

Cardiometabolic Outcomes: Greater reductions in waist circumference, BP, fasting lipids, and fasting glucose with liraglutide; reduced prediabetes prevalence and delayed type 2 diabetes onset.

Safety Profile: Most common AEs: mild-moderate nausea and diarrhea; gallbladder events and resting pulse slightly higher. Liraglutide 3.0 mg is a safe, effective adjunct to diet and exercise for weight management.
keyPoints: ↓ BP, lipids, prediabetes; delayed T2DM onset
Nausea most common AE; gallbladder events 2.5% vs 1.0%
items.reference: Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: Why was liraglutide specifically evaluated at a 3.0 mg dose instead of the 1.8 mg dose approved for diabetes?
items.answer: Weight loss with liraglutide is dose-dependent up to 3.0 mg once daily, making this higher dose the target for specific weight-management interventions.
keyPoints: Weight loss with liraglutide is dose-dependent up to 3.0 mg once daily, making this higher dose the target for specific weight-management interventions.
items.reference: SCALE Q1: Weight loss with liraglutide is dose-dependent up to 3.0 mg once daily, making t... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: How does liraglutide functionally induce weight loss in humans?
items.answer: The weight loss is mediated physiologically by reduced appetite and energy intake, rather than by an increase in resting energy expenditure.
keyPoints: The weight loss is mediated physiologically by reduced appetite and energy intake, rather than by an increase in resting energy expenditure.
items.reference: SCALE Q2: The weight loss is mediated physiologically by reduced appetite and energy intak... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: Why did the researchers evaluate patients with a BMI between 27 and 29.9?
items.answer: Patients with a BMI in the overweight category (27 to 29.9) were included because they carried concurrent weight-related comorbidities, specifically treated or untreated dyslipidemia or hypertension, placing them at higher clinical risk.
keyPoints: Patients with a BMI in the overweight category (27 to 29.9) were included because they carried concurrent weight-related comorbidities, specifically treated or untreated dyslipidemia or hypertension, placing them at higher clinical risk.
items.reference: SCALE Q3: Patients with a BMI in the overweight category (27 to 29.9) were included becaus... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: How was the primary prespecified efficacy data mathematically handled when a patient dropped out?
items.answer: Missing values were imputed using the last-observation-carried-forward method for measurements made after baseline.
keyPoints: Missing values were imputed using the last-observation-carried-forward method for measurements made after baseline.
items.reference: SCALE Q4: Missing values were imputed using the last-observation-carried-forward method fo... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: Why was a specific subgroup of the liraglutide cohort randomly switched to a placebo after 56 weeks?
items.answer: This 12-week switch was designed to assess whether the drug's efficacy was maintained after discontinuation and to determine if there were any adverse safety issues related to treatment cessation.
keyPoints: This 12-week switch was designed to assess whether the drug's efficacy was maintained after discontinuation and to determine if there were any adverse safety issues related to treatment cessation.
items.reference: SCALE Q5: This 12-week switch was designed to assess whether the drug's efficacy was maint... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: How did the intervention impact the development of new-onset type 2 diabetes over the 56 weeks?
items.answer: Type 2 diabetes developed in significantly more patients in the placebo group than in the liraglutide group, indicating that the weight loss and improved glycemic control actively delayed disease onset.
keyPoints: Type 2 diabetes developed in significantly more patients in the placebo group than in the liraglutide group, indicating that the weight loss and improved glycemic control actively delayed disease onset.
items.reference: SCALE Q6: Type 2 diabetes developed in significantly more patients in the placebo group th... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: Why were gallbladder-related events (like cholelithiasis) noted as a specific safety concern in the liraglutide group?
items.answer: Gallbladder events occurred more frequently in the liraglutide group (2.5% vs. 1.0%), which is consistent with the known pathophysiological risk of developing gallstones during rapid or substantial weight loss.
keyPoints: Gallbladder events occurred more frequently in the liraglutide group (2.5% vs. 1.0%), which is consistent with the known pathophysiological risk of developing gallstones during rapid or substantial weight loss.
items.reference: SCALE Q7: Gallbladder events occurred more frequently in the liraglutide group (2.5% vs. 1... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: How did the treatment affect patients' cardiovascular vital signs over the study period?
items.answer: Liraglutide significantly reduced both systolic and diastolic blood pressure, but it simultaneously caused a slight increase in the mean resting pulse (an estimated difference of 2.4 beats/min over placebo).
keyPoints: Liraglutide significantly reduced both systolic and diastolic blood pressure, but it simultaneously caused a slight increase in the mean resting pulse (an estimated difference of 2.4 beats/min over placebo).
items.reference: SCALE Q8: Liraglutide significantly reduced both systolic and diastolic blood pressure, bu... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: Why do the authors hypothesize that the increased resting pulse is not necessarily dangerous?
items.answer: While the exact clinical relevance is unknown, it is likely a direct chronotropic effect related to the presence of GLP-1 receptors directly on the sinoatrial node, which is a known characteristic of this drug class.
keyPoints: While the exact clinical relevance is unknown, it is likely a direct chronotropic effect related to the presence of GLP-1 receptors directly on the sinoatrial node, which is a known characteristic of this drug class.
items.reference: SCALE Q9: While the exact clinical relevance is unknown, it is likely a direct chronotropi... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: How did the investigators quantify the coprimary efficacy endpoints for body weight?
items.answer: They measured the absolute and percentage change in body weight, the proportion of patients losing at least 5% of their initial weight, and the proportion losing more than 10% of their initial weight.
keyPoints: They measured the absolute and percentage change in body weight, the proportion of patients losing at least 5% of their initial weight, and the proportion losing more than 10% of their initial weight.
items.reference: SCALE Q10: They measured the absolute and percentage change in body weight, the proportion ... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: Why did the authors conclude that isolated pancreatic enzyme elevations are poor indicators of acute pancreatitis in these patients?
items.answer: Although mean lipase and amylase activity increased in the liraglutide group, the positive predictive value of isolated enzyme elevations was very low (less than 1% for a lipase value ≥3 times the upper limit of normal).
keyPoints: Although mean lipase and amylase activity increased in the liraglutide group, the positive predictive value of isolated enzyme elevations was very low (less than 1% for a lipase value ≥3 times the upper limit of normal).
items.reference: SCALE Q11: Although mean lipase and amylase activity increased in the liraglutide group, th... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: How did baseline prediabetes status affect a patient's total weight loss on liraglutide?
items.answer: It had no significant effect; weight loss with liraglutide was maintained over 56 weeks and was similar regardless of a patient's prediabetes status.
keyPoints: It had no significant effect; weight loss with liraglutide was maintained over 56 weeks and was similar regardless of a patient's prediabetes status.
items.reference: SCALE Q12: It had no significant effect; weight loss with liraglutide was maintained over 5... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: Why was the overall dropout rate slightly higher for patients taking liraglutide compared to the placebo?
items.answer: A larger percentage of patients in the liraglutide group withdrew from the trial owing to adverse events (9.9% vs. 3.8%), which were primarily mild or moderate gastrointestinal disorders.
keyPoints: A larger percentage of patients in the liraglutide group withdrew from the trial owing to adverse events (9.9% vs. 3.8%), which were primarily mild or moderate gastrointestinal disorders.
items.reference: SCALE Q13: A larger percentage of patients in the liraglutide group withdrew from the trial... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: How were patients medically transitioned to the target 3.0 mg dose to mitigate early side effects?
items.answer: Patients were started at a low dose of 0.6 mg, with weekly 0.6-mg increments added until they reached the 3.0 mg therapeutic target.
keyPoints: Patients were started at a low dose of 0.6 mg, with weekly 0.6-mg increments added until they reached the 3.0 mg therapeutic target.
items.reference: SCALE Q14: Patients were started at a low dose of 0.6 mg, with weekly 0.6-mg increments add... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: Why were calcitonin concentrations carefully monitored throughout the safety analysis?
items.answer: Because GLP-1 receptor agonists have been observed to cause C-cell proliferation in rodents, researchers monitored calcitonin to ensure there was no increased risk of medullary thyroid carcinoma in humans (no cases or calcitonin elevations were observed).
keyPoints: Because GLP-1 receptor agonists have been observed to cause C-cell proliferation in rodents, researchers monitored calcitonin to ensure there was no increased risk of medullary thyroid carcinoma in humans (no cases or calcitonin elevations were observed).
items.reference: SCALE Q15: Because GLP-1 receptor agonists have been observed to cause C-cell proliferation... — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: What was the randomization ratio between the liraglutide group and the placebo group?
items.explanation: Randomization was 2:1 (liraglutide:placebo).
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 1: Randomization was 2:1 (liraglutide:placebo). — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: Which of the following pre-existing conditions explicitly excluded a patient from participating in the trial?
options: Prediabetes
Untreated dyslipidemia
Type 1 or 2 diabetes
Hypertension
items.explanation: Patients with type 1 or 2 diabetes were excluded.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 2: Patients with type 1 or 2 diabetes were excluded. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: What percentage of the overall randomized trial population was female?
items.explanation: 78.5% of participants were female.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 3: 78.5% of participants were female. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: What was the mean change in body weight (in kilograms) for the liraglutide group at week 56?
items.explanation: Mean weight change was -8.4 kg in the liraglutide group.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 4: Mean weight change was -8.4 kg in the liraglutide group. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: What percentage of patients in the liraglutide group successfully lost at least 5% of their body weight?
items.explanation: 63.2% of liraglutide patients lost ≥5% body weight.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 5: 63.2% of liraglutide patients lost ≥5% body weight. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: According to the adverse event data, which of the following was the most common side effect reported by patients taking liraglutide?
options: Constipation
Diarrhea
Vomiting
Nausea
items.explanation: Nausea was the most common adverse event with liraglutide.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 6: Nausea was the most common adverse event with liraglutide. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: What was the most frequent reason that patients in the placebo group withdrew from the trial?
options: Severe adverse events
Ineffective therapy
Withdrawal of consent
Development of symptomatic diabetes
items.explanation: Withdrawal of consent was the most common reason in the placebo group.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 7: Withdrawal of consent was the most common reason in the placebo group. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: During what time frame did nausea and vomiting primarily manifest for patients taking liraglutide?
options: Consistently throughout the entire 56 weeks
Within the first 4 to 8 weeks after initiation
Only after the 24-week mark
Immediately upon discontinuation of the drug
items.explanation: Nausea/vomiting peaked in the first 4–8 weeks during dose escalation.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 8: Nausea/vomiting peaked in the first 4–8 weeks during dose escalation. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: How many confirmed cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma or C-cell hyperplasia were observed during the trial?
items.explanation: No medullary thyroid carcinoma or C-cell hyperplasia cases were observed.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 9: No medullary thyroid carcinoma or C-cell hyperplasia cases were observed. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: What was the estimated treatment difference in systolic blood pressure between the liraglutide group and the placebo group?
options: -1.5 mm Hg
-2.8 mm Hg
-4.2 mm Hg
+2.4 mm Hg
items.explanation: Systolic BP was 2.8 mm Hg lower with liraglutide vs placebo.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 10: Systolic BP was 2.8 mm Hg lower with liraglutide vs placebo. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: How did liraglutide impact fasting glucose levels compared to placebo (estimated treatment difference)?
options: -2.3 mg/dl
-6.9 mg/dl
0.1 mg/dl
-12.6 mg/dl
items.explanation: Fasting glucose was 6.9 mg/dl lower with liraglutide.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 11: Fasting glucose was 6.9 mg/dl lower with liraglutide. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: What proportion of patients in the total trial population met the criteria for prediabetes at baseline?
items.explanation: 61.2% had prediabetes at baseline.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 12: 61.2% had prediabetes at baseline. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: Which established survey instrument was utilized to assess overall physical and mental health quality of life?
options: Treatment Related Impact Measure–Weight
SF-36
Impact of Weight on Quality of Life–Lite
Beck Depression Inventory
items.explanation: SF-36 assessed physical and mental health quality of life.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 13: SF-36 assessed physical and mental health quality of life. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: What percentage of patients taking liraglutide reported gallbladder-related events (such as cholelithiasis)?
items.explanation: Gallbladder-related events occurred in 2.5% of liraglutide patients.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 14: Gallbladder-related events occurred in 2.5% of liraglutide patients. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: How did fasting insulin levels change relative to baseline in the liraglutide group?
options: They increased by 12.6%
They decreased by 4.4%
They decreased by 12.6%
They remained statistically unchanged
items.explanation: Fasting insulin decreased 12.6% from baseline with liraglutide.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 15: Fasting insulin decreased 12.6% from baseline with liraglutide. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: What was the estimated treatment difference in waist circumference reduction favoring the liraglutide group?
items.explanation: Waist circumference was 4.2 cm lower with liraglutide vs placebo.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 16: Waist circumference was 4.2 cm lower with liraglutide vs placebo. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: How did liraglutide affect the patients' mean resting pulse?
options: It significantly lowered resting pulse by 4 beats/min.
It caused an increase of 2.4 beats/min over placebo.
It triggered severe tachycardia requiring hospitalization in 8.7% of patients.
It had no measurable effect on resting pulse.
items.explanation: Resting pulse increased 2.4 beats/min over placebo with liraglutide.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 17: Resting pulse increased 2.4 beats/min over placebo with liraglutide. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: According to the subgroup analysis, which specific patient demographic appeared to have a slightly less effective weight loss response to liraglutide?
options: Patients with prediabetes
Patients with a BMI of 40 or higher
Patients with pre-existing hypertension
Female patients
items.explanation: Weight loss was slightly less in patients with BMI ≥40.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 18: Weight loss was slightly less in patients with BMI ≥40. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: What was the total number of patients randomly assigned across both groups in this trial?
items.explanation: 3,731 patients were randomized.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 19: 3,731 patients were randomized. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.question: A numerical imbalance was observed in the trial regarding the incidence of which type of neoplasm?
options: Malignant melanoma
Pancreatic cancer
Malignant and pre-malignant breast neoplasms
Colorectal cancer
items.explanation: Numerical imbalance noted for malignant and pre-malignant breast neoplasms.
items.reference: SCALE MCQ 20: Numerical imbalance noted for malignant and pre-malignant breast neoplasms. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: The trial criteria explicitly excluded patients who had a history of major depressive or other severe psychiatric disorders.
items.explanation: Major depressive or severe psychiatric disorders were exclusion criteria.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 1: Major depressive or severe psychiatric disorders were exclusion criteria. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: Previous mechanistic studies show that liraglutide stimulates weight loss primarily by dramatically increasing the body's resting energy expenditure.
items.explanation: Weight loss is mediated by reduced appetite/energy intake, not increased resting energy expenditure.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 2: Weight loss is mediated by reduced appetite/energy intake, not increased resting energy expenditure. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: Patients taking liraglutide were significantly more likely to report severe spontaneous hypoglycemia that required third-party assistance compared to the placebo group.
items.explanation: Severe spontaneous hypoglycemia was not significantly more frequent with liraglutide.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 3: Severe spontaneous hypoglycemia was not significantly more frequent with liraglutide. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: The prespecified primary analyses utilized the last-observation-carried-forward (LOCF) imputation method to account for missing data from patients who dropped out.
items.explanation: Primary analyses used LOCF imputation for missing post-baseline data.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 4: Primary analyses used LOCF imputation for missing post-baseline data. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: Approximately 92% of the patients assigned to the liraglutide group experienced at least some measurable degree of weight loss.
items.explanation: ~92% of liraglutide patients had some weight loss.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 5: ~92% of liraglutide patients had some weight loss. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: Liraglutide was associated with a statistically significant increase in systolic blood pressure over the 56-week trial.
items.explanation: Liraglutide significantly reduced systolic blood pressure.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 6: Liraglutide significantly reduced systolic blood pressure. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: The vast majority of patients who developed cholelithiasis or cholecystitis underwent elective cholecystectomy and successfully continued their assigned study treatment.
items.explanation: Most gallbladder-event patients had cholecystectomy and continued study treatment.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 7: Most gallbladder-event patients had cholecystectomy and continued study treatment. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: To be eligible for the study, a patient had to have a baseline BMI of 35 or higher.
items.explanation: Eligibility was BMI ≥30, or ≥27 with dyslipidemia or hypertension.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 8: Eligibility was BMI ≥30, or ≥27 with dyslipidemia or hypertension. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: The sponsor of the trial, Novo Nordisk, planned the statistical analyses, provided the trial drugs, and provided editorial assistance.
items.explanation: Novo Nordisk sponsored the trial, planned analyses, supplied drug, and provided editorial assistance.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 9: Novo Nordisk sponsored the trial, planned analyses, supplied drug, and provided editorial assistance. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: On the Treatment Related Impact Measure–Weight questionnaire, the liraglutide group reported a lower (worse) score regarding the experience of side effects compared to the placebo group.
items.explanation: Liraglutide group reported worse side-effect experience scores on TRIM-Weight.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 10: Liraglutide group reported worse side-effect experience scores on TRIM-Weight. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: The data showed that patients who experienced gallbladder-related events generally had below-average weight loss.
items.explanation: Gallbladder-event patients generally had above-average weight loss.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 11: Gallbladder-event patients generally had above-average weight loss. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: Of the confirmed acute pancreatitis cases in the liraglutide group, half were directly associated with gallstones.
items.explanation: Half of confirmed pancreatitis cases in liraglutide group were gallstone-associated.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 12: Half of confirmed pancreatitis cases in liraglutide group were gallstone-associated. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: Patients assigned to the placebo group did not receive any counseling on lifestyle modification, diet, or physical activity during the trial.
items.explanation: All patients received standard lifestyle-modification counseling.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 13: All patients received standard lifestyle-modification counseling. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: Measurements taken during the oral glucose-tolerance test showed that liraglutide improved beta-cell function and insulin sensitivity more effectively than the placebo.
items.explanation: OGTT showed improved beta-cell function and insulin sensitivity with liraglutide.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 14: OGTT showed improved beta-cell function and insulin sensitivity with liraglutide. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.statement: Treatment cessation at the end of the 56-week main trial period was strongly associated with severe rebound binge eating.
items.explanation: No strong association with severe rebound binge eating after treatment cessation.
items.reference: SCALE T/F 15: No strong association with severe rebound binge eating after treatment cessation. — Pi-Sunyer X, Astrup A, Fujioka K, et al. A randomized, controlled trial of 3.0 mg of liraglutide in weight management. N Engl J Med 2015;373:11-22.

items.stem: Why was liraglutide specifically evaluated at a 3.0 mg dose instead of the 1.8 mg dose approved for diabetes?

B) The 3.0 mg dose has superior glycemic control compared to the 1.8 mg dose.
C) The 1.8 mg dose is contraindicated in patients with obesity.
D) Regulatory approval for diabetes requires evaluation at the 3.0 mg dose.
items.explanation: Weight loss with liraglutide is dose-dependent up to 3.0 mg once daily, making this higher dose the target for specific weight-management interventions.

items.stem: How were missing values in the primary prespecified efficacy data handled when patients dropped out of the study?
options: A. Missing values were imputed using the last-observation-carried-forward method for measurements made after baseline.
B. Missing values were excluded from the analysis entirely.

items.explanation: Last-observation-carried-forward (LOCF) is a traditional method for handling missing post-baseline data in clinical trials, though it has limitations in bias and variability.

items.stem: Why did the authors conclude that isolated pancreatic enzyme elevations are poor indicators of acute pancreatitis in these patients?
options: A. Although mean lipase and amylase activity increased in the liraglutide group, the positive predictive value of isolated enzyme elevations was very low (less than 1% for a lipase value ≥3 times the upper limit of normal).
B. Pancreatic enzyme levels did not correlate with the severity of gastrointestinal symptoms in patients taking liraglutide.
C. Imaging studies showed no evidence of pancreatic inflammation in patients with elevated enzyme levels.
D. Enzyme elevations were not observed in patients with confirmed acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Low positive predictive value indicates that isolated enzyme elevations are not reliable for diagnosing acute pancreatitis, as most patients with elevated enzymes do not have the condition.

items.stem: Why was the overall dropout rate slightly higher for patients taking liraglutide compared to the placebo?
options: A. A larger percentage of patients in the liraglutide group withdrew from the trial owing to adverse events (9.9% vs. 3.8%), which were primarily mild or moderate gastrointestinal disorders.
B. Liraglutide demonstrated greater efficacy, leading to early discontinuation by patients achieving target outcomes.
C. Patients in the liraglutide group preferred the placebo and requested to discontinue the trial.
D. Severe cardiovascular adverse events were more frequently reported in the liraglutide group.
items.explanation: Higher dropout rates in the liraglutide group were attributed to a greater incidence of mild or moderate gastrointestinal adverse events, a known side effect of GLP-1 receptor agonists.

items.stem: How were patients medically transitioned to the target 3.0 mg dose to mitigate early side effects?
options: A: Patients were started at a low dose of 0.6 mg, with weekly 0.6-mg increments added until they reached the 3.0 mg therapeutic target.
B: Patients were started at a higher dose of 1.5 mg and titrated downward to 3.0 mg over 2 weeks.
C: Patients received a fixed dose of 3.0 mg immediately, with no incremental adjustments.
D: Patients were started at 0.6 mg and increased by 1.2 mg weekly until reaching 3.0 mg.
items.explanation: Gradual titration from a low initial dose with incremental increases minimizes the risk of early side effects associated with rapid dose escalation.
